terms.definition: A public and private keypair that gives you access to, and control over, the tokens held under a specific address. The blockchain itself keeps track of what tokens are held by each account; the tokens aren't literally "in" the wallet, but we talk that way because the metaphor makes sense. On Ethereum-compatible blockchains, a single Secret Recovery Phrase can generate a near-infinite number of accounts.

A string used to send and receive transactions on a blockchain network and to identify different users; also called a 'public key'. An address is an alphanumeric string that can also be shown as a scannable QR code. On Ethereum, addresses begin with 0x, for example 0x06A8…08ee.

A method of securing a device by keeping it fully disconnected from the internet and any open network. Many hardware wallets use air-gapping so a user's private keys or Secret Recovery Phrase never touch an online machine, making them far harder to attack.

A distribution of tokens sent directly to many wallet addresses, usually for free. Projects use airdrops to reward early users, decentralise ownership, or bootstrap a community. Always verify an airdrop is genuine before interacting with it — fake airdrops are a common scam.
Any cryptocurrency other than Bitcoin. The term is short for 'alternative coin' and covers everything from large networks like Ethereum and Solana to thousands of smaller tokens.

A type of decentralised exchange that prices assets using a mathematical formula and pooled liquidity rather than a traditional order book. Users trade against the pool, and liquidity providers earn a share of the fees.

Two ways of expressing the yield on an asset over a year. APR (Annual Percentage Rate) is the simple rate; APY (Annual Percentage Yield) includes the effect of compounding. In DeFi, advertised APYs can change quickly as conditions shift.

A sustained period of falling prices and pessimistic sentiment across a market. The opposite of a bull market. In crypto, bear markets can be deep and prolonged, which is why education and risk management matter.
The coordination layer that introduced proof-of-stake to Ethereum. It manages validators and consensus, and was merged with Ethereum's main execution layer during 'The Merge' in 2022.

The first cryptocurrency, launched in 2009 by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto. Bitcoin is a decentralised, peer-to-peer digital money secured by proof-of-work mining, with a fixed maximum supply of 21 million coins.
A batch of validated transactions bundled together and added to a blockchain. Each block references the one before it, forming an unbroken, tamper-evident chain of history.
A search tool for a blockchain. Block explorers let anyone look up transactions, addresses, blocks, and token balances in real time — a transparent public record of on-chain activity.
The position of a block in the chain, counted as the number of blocks before it. The genesis (first) block has a height of zero, and the number rises by one with every new block.
The tokens paid to the miner or validator who adds a new block to the chain. The reward, plus transaction fees, is what incentivises participants to secure the network.
A sustained period of rising prices and optimistic sentiment. The opposite of a bear market. Strong narratives, new capital, and growing adoption often drive crypto bull markets.
Permanently removing tokens from circulation by sending them to an address no one can access. Projects burn tokens to reduce supply, which can support scarcity-driven value over time.

A wallet that stores private keys completely offline, such as a hardware device or paper backup. Because it isn't connected to the internet, a cold wallet is much safer for long-term holdings than a hot wallet.
The set of rules a blockchain uses to agree on which transactions are valid and in what order, without a central authority. Proof-of-work and proof-of-stake are the two most common mechanisms.
A digital asset secured by cryptography and recorded on a blockchain. Cryptocurrencies can be transferred peer-to-peer without a bank or central intermediary.
A Decentralised Autonomous Organisation — a community-run entity governed by rules encoded in smart contracts and by token-holder voting, rather than by traditional management.
A decentralised application that runs on a blockchain through smart contracts rather than on a single company's servers. DApps span finance, gaming, social, and more.
Decentralised Finance — financial services like lending, borrowing, trading, and earning yield built on public blockchains using smart contracts, with no central intermediary.
A Decentralised Exchange that lets users trade tokens directly from their own wallets via smart contracts, without handing custody of funds to a central platform.
A decentralised blockchain that introduced programmable smart contracts, enabling DApps, DeFi, NFTs, and more. Its native token is Ether (ETH), used to pay for transactions.
The Ethereum Virtual Machine — the runtime that executes smart contracts on Ethereum. Many other chains are 'EVM-compatible', meaning they can run the same contracts and tools.

Government-issued currency such as the Naira, US Dollar, or Euro that is not backed by a physical commodity. In crypto, 'fiat' usually refers to the traditional money you exchange in and out of digital assets.
A change to a blockchain's rules. A soft fork is backward-compatible; a hard fork creates a permanent split, sometimes producing a new chain and token.
The fee paid to process a transaction or run a smart contract on a blockchain. Gas compensates validators for computation and rises when the network is busy.
A small denomination of Ether used to measure gas prices. One Gwei equals one-billionth of an ETH, which makes fee amounts easier to read.
A scheduled event that cuts a cryptocurrency's block reward in half. Bitcoin halves roughly every four years, gradually slowing the creation of new supply.

A fixed-length string produced by running data through a cryptographic function. The same input always gives the same hash, and even a tiny change produces a completely different one — which is how blockchains stay tamper-evident.
A wallet connected to the internet, such as a browser extension or mobile app. Hot wallets are convenient for everyday use but more exposed than cold wallets, so keep only what you need in them.
An Initial Coin Offering — a fundraising method where a project sells a new token to early supporters. ICOs can be high-risk and lightly regulated, so research carefully before participating.
Unchangeable once recorded. Confirmed blockchain transactions are immutable: they can't be edited or deleted, which is central to the trust and transparency of the system.
Know Your Customer — the identity-verification process many exchanges and services require to comply with regulations and reduce fraud.
A network built on top of a base blockchain (Layer 1) to make transactions faster and cheaper, then settle back to the main chain for security. Rollups like Arbitrum and Optimism are common examples.
How easily an asset can be bought or sold without sharply moving its price. Deep liquidity means tighter spreads and smoother trades; thin liquidity means higher slippage.
The live, public version of a blockchain where real transactions with real value take place — as opposed to a testnet used for development.
The total value of a cryptocurrency, calculated by multiplying its current price by the number of coins in circulation. It's a quick way to gauge a project's relative size.
The process of validating transactions and adding new blocks in a proof-of-work network by solving computational puzzles. Miners are rewarded with new coins and fees for securing the chain.
A Non-Fungible Token — a unique on-chain token that represents ownership of a specific item such as art, collectibles, music, or in-game assets. Unlike coins, each NFT is one of a kind.
A computer that runs a blockchain's software, helping to store, verify, and relay its data. A large, distributed set of nodes is what keeps a network decentralised and resilient.
A 'number used once'. In mining it's the value miners adjust to find a valid block hash; in accounts it's a counter that orders your transactions and prevents replays.
A service that feeds real-world data — like prices, weather, or sports results — into smart contracts, which can't access off-chain information on their own.

Direct interaction between two parties without an intermediary. LoopBridge's WhatsApp Exchange is a P2P model: you trade with a counterparty, supported by a human, rather than through a central order book.
The secret number that controls an account and authorises its transactions. Anyone with your private key controls your funds, so it must never be shared. Whoever holds the keys holds the crypto.

A consensus mechanism where validators lock up (stake) tokens for the right to confirm blocks. Honest validation earns rewards; dishonest behaviour can lose the stake. PoS uses far less energy than proof-of-work.
A consensus mechanism where miners compete to solve computational puzzles to add blocks. It is highly secure but energy-intensive, and it is the system that originally secured Bitcoin.
The shareable counterpart to your private key, from which your wallet address is derived. You can give out your public key or address to receive funds safely.
The smallest unit of Bitcoin, equal to one hundred-millionth of a BTC. It's named after Bitcoin's pseudonymous creator, Satoshi Nakamoto.

A list of usually 12 or 24 words that backs up a wallet and can restore all of its accounts. Also called a Secret Recovery Phrase or mnemonic. Store it offline and never share it — anyone with it controls your funds.
Self-executing code on a blockchain that runs automatically when its conditions are met. Smart contracts power DeFi, NFTs, DAOs, and most other on-chain applications.
A cryptocurrency designed to hold a steady value, usually pegged to a fiat currency like the US Dollar. Examples include USDT and USDC, often used to move value and shelter from volatility.
Locking up tokens to help secure a proof-of-stake network and earn rewards in return. Staking can be done directly as a validator or through pools and services.
A sandbox version of a blockchain that uses valueless tokens, letting developers test apps and contracts safely before deploying to mainnet.
A digital asset issued on top of an existing blockchain, often via a smart contract. Tokens can represent currency, governance rights, access, or ownership of real or digital things.
Any action recorded on a blockchain, such as sending tokens or interacting with a smart contract. Once confirmed, a transaction is permanent and publicly verifiable.
A participant in a proof-of-stake network who stakes tokens to propose and confirm blocks. Validators keep the chain secure and earn rewards for honest participation.
How sharply and quickly an asset's price moves. Crypto is known for high volatility, which creates both opportunity and risk — a key reason to trade responsibly.
Software or hardware that stores your keys and lets you send, receive, and manage crypto. A wallet doesn't actually hold coins; it holds the keys that prove ownership of assets on-chain.
The next phase of the internet built on blockchains, where users own their data, identity, and assets directly rather than relying on centralised platforms. It's the foundation of New Finance.
An individual or entity that holds a very large amount of a cryptocurrency. Whales can move markets when they buy or sell, so their activity is often watched closely.
Moving crypto between DeFi protocols to earn the best possible returns from interest, fees, and incentives. It can be lucrative but carries smart-contract and market risks.
